agent1_snippets.title: Biochemistry, C Peptide -- Issues of Concern
agent1_snippets.content: The normal physiological C-peptide plasma concentration in a fasted state is 0.9 to 1.8 ng/ml. [1] A high level could indicate insulin resistance, insulinoma, or kidney disease. A low C-peptide is usually present in patients with type 1, or sometimes, type 2 diabetes.

agent1_snippets.title: Insulin Resistance -- Complications
agent1_snippets.content: Non-alcoholic fatty liver disease (NAFLD) is intricately related to insulin resistance and T2D. Patients with T2D have a 2-fold increased risk for NAFLD. With an increasing worldwide prevalence and incidence in children, NAFLD should be of great concern to clinicians treating patients with insulin resistance. [19] [20]

agent1_snippets.title: Insulin Resistance -- Continuing Education Activity
agent1_snippets.content: Insulin resistance, identified as an impaired biologic response to insulin stimulation of target tissues, primarily involves liver, muscle, and adipose tissue. Insulin resistance impairs glucose disposal, resulting in a compensatory increase in beta-cell insulin production and hyperinsulinemia. The metabolic consequences of insulin resistance can result in hyperglycemia, hypertension, dyslipidemia, hyperuricemia, elevated inflammatory markers, endothelial dysfunction, and a prothrombotic state. The predominant consequence of insulin resistance is type 2 diabetes(T2D). Insulin resistance is thought to precede the development of T2D by 10 to 15 years. Lifestyle modifications should be the primary focus when treating insulin resistance. Nutritional intervention with calorie reduction and avoidance of carbohydrates that stimulate excessive insulin demand is a cornerstone of treatment. Physical activity helps to increase energy expenditure and improve skeletal muscle insulin sensitivity. Medications also can improve insulin response and reduce insulin demand. Most of the complications from insulin resistance are related to the development of vascular complications and nonalcoholic fatty liver disease. This activity reviews the etiology, pathogenesis, epidemiology, presentation, treatment, and potential complications of insulin resistance and highlights the crucial role of the interprofessional team in its management.

agent1_snippets.title: Insulin Resistance -- Introduction
agent1_snippets.content: Insulin resistance is identified as the impaired biologic response of target tissues to insulin stimulation. All tissues with insulin receptors can become insulin resistant, but the tissues that primarily drive insulin resistance are the liver, skeletal muscle, and adipose tissue. Insulin resistance impairs glucose disposal, resulting in a compensatory increase in beta-cell insulin production and hyperinsulinemia. Recent studies have debated whether hyperinsulinemia precedes insulin resistance, as hyperinsulinemia itself is a driver of insulin resistance. This concept may be clinically valuable, suggesting that hyperinsulinemia associated with excess caloric intake may drive the metabolic dysfunction associated with insulin resistance. The metabolic consequences of insulin resistance include hyperglycemia, hypertension, dyslipidemia, hyperuricemia, elevated inflammatory markers, endothelial dysfunction, and a prothrombotic state. Progression of insulin resistance can lead to metabolic syndrome, nonalcoholic fatty liver disease (NAFLD), and type 2 diabetes. [1] [2] [3] [4] [5]

agent1_snippets.title: Hepatocellular Carcinoma  -- Deterrence and Patient Education
agent1_snippets.content: Hepatocellular carcinoma is the fifth leading cause of cancer worldwide and the second cause of cancer deaths in men. Risk factors for HCC include chronic hepatitis B virus infection, chronic hepatitis C virus infection, alcohol abuse, and non-alcoholic fatty liver disease. NAFLD is now a leading cause of HCC worldwide, especially in western countries. This is due to the increase in metabolic syndrome. Metabolic syndrome occurs in patients with insulin resistance, hypertension, hypertriglyceridemia, and abdominal obesity, which increases cardiovascular risk. HCC is diagnosed by elevation of serum biomarkers, including alpha-fetoprotein, imaging including ultrasound, contrast CT/MRI, and biopsy. Five-year survival of HCC is 18% and second to pancreatic cancer.

agent1_snippets.title: Biochemistry, Very Low Density Lipoprotein -- Clinical Significance
agent1_snippets.content: VLDL cholesterol is also associated with hepatic disorders such as non-alcoholic fatty liver disease (NAFLD) and hepatitis. Liver fat content has been previously associated with other features of chronic insulin resistance, such as glucose intolerance, increased insulin levels, and intra-abdominal fat. [2] In subjects with NAFLD, there is increased hydrolysis of intrahepatic triglycerides, leading to increased secretion of VLDL. Patients with NAFLD also have an altered synthesis, oxidation, and reduction of VLDL particles due to the impaired effects of insulin. [31] It has been theorized that the lessened effects of insulin lead to an increase in lipid deposition from adipose tissue to the liver. Elevations in blood glucose can further increase fat accumulation in the liver, resulting in enhanced production of VLDL and leading to the expected hyperlipidemia associated with diabetes. [2]

agent1_snippets.title: Factitious Hypoglycemia -- Pathophysiology
agent1_snippets.content: Insulin synthesis occurs in the β-cells of the pancreas. Preproinsulin secreted from β-cells is cleaved into proinsulin, which is then converted into insulin and C peptide. [4] Insulin and c peptide are stored in secretory granules and will be released in the event of hyperglycemia. The liver will rapidly remove the circulating insulin, whereas C peptide remains in the circulation for a more extended period of time until cleared renally. The insulin to C peptide ratio in healthy individuals is usually less than one. A small quantity of proinsulin is also secreted into blood circulation without processing, which is detectable in the plasma. [4]

agent1_snippets.title: Biochemistry, C Peptide -- Clinical Significance
agent1_snippets.content: The other major indication is the assessment of insulin secretory reserve in patients with diabetes. Diabetes mellitus is characterized by hyperglycemia due to the lack of insulin secretion and/or insulin action. Insulin deficiency is associated with C-peptide-deficiency in type 1 diabetes due to beta-cell demise. A fasting C-peptide level of less than 0.6 ng/ml is consistent with beta-cell failure and predicts the requirement for insulin therapy. Although the origins of type 2 diabetes are insulin resistance, it only manifests clinically when there is beta-cell failure resulting in impaired insulin and C-peptide secretion culminating in fasting and postprandial hyperglycemia. Much more research is needed to define the biology of C-peptide and potential role in the pathogenesis of diabetic microvascular complications or as a novel therapeutic agent.

agent1_snippets.title: Metabolic Dysfunction-Associated Steatotic Liver Disease (MΑSLD) -- Evaluation -- Assessment of comorbidities
agent1_snippets.content: Comprehensive comorbidity assessment requires systematic evaluation across multiple domains, with specific reference parameters and supplementary investigations tailored to individual clinical presentations. For obesity assessment, primary parameters include body mass index, waist circumference, and waist-to-height ratio, with additional body composition analysis and thyroid function evaluation (TSH and free thyroxine) when hypothyroidism is suspected. Type 2 diabetes and insulin resistance evaluation encompasses fasting plasma glucose, HbA1c, oral glucose tolerance testing with a 2-hour postload glucose measurement, fasting plasma insulin and C-peptide levels, and HOMA-IR calculation.

agent1_snippets.title: Lipodystrophies -- Pertinent Studies and Ongoing Trials
agent1_snippets.content: Lack of adipose tissue results in marked hypoleptinemia in patients with lipodystrophy. These patients have an increased risk of difficult to treat metabolic disorders including hypertriglyceridemia, marked insulin resistance, type 2 diabetes, recurrent pancreatitis, fatty liver disease, NASH, and liver fibrosis [20] Various trials have been carried out to study the efficacy of Human recombinant leptin (metreleptin) in patients with lipodystrophy.

agent1_snippets.title: Secondary Causes of Obesity and Comprehensive Diagnostic Evaluation -- Function -- Lipodystrophy Diagnostic Criteria
agent1_snippets.content: Biochemical markers often reveal severe insulin resistance with fasting insulin levels exceeding 30 μU/mL. Triglyceride levels typically surpass 500 mg/dL. Serum leptin concentrations fall below 4 ng/mL, but must be interpreted in conjunction with overall fat mass and metabolic status. Elevated liver enzymes often signal hepatic steatosis. Imaging techniques, such as magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) or dual-energy x-ray absorptiometry (DEXA) scanning, help quantify and map fat distribution, supporting diagnosis and disease monitoring. Please refer to the StatPearls' companion resource, " Lipodystrophy ," for additional information.

agent1_snippets.title: Insulin Resistance -- Pathophysiology -- Adipose Tissue
agent1_snippets.content: Using the hyperinsulinemic-euglycemic clamp technique, researchers determined that lipolysis is sensitive to insulin. The failure of insulin to suppress lipolysis in insulin-resistant adipose tissue, especially visceral adipose tissue, increases circulating free fatty acids (FFAs). Higher levels of circulating FFAs directly affect both liver and muscle metabolism, further exacerbating insulin resistance in these tissues and contributing to lipotoxicity-induced beta-cell dysfunction. [25] [30]

agent1_snippets.title: Somogyi Phenomenon -- Issues of Concern -- Insulin Release and Insulin Resistance
agent1_snippets.content: In an individual that does not have diabetes, the blood glucose and insulin concentrations stay flat and constant throughout the night, with a transient increase in insulin just before dawn to prevent hepatic glucose production through gluconeogenesis and prevent hyperglycemia. [5] This explains why non-diabetic patients do not exhibit the dawn phenomenon, as their insulin levels follow the circadian pattern necessary for optimal glucose control.

agent1_snippets.title: Insulin Resistance -- Pathophysiology -- Hepatic Tissue
agent1_snippets.content: The liver is responsible for processing energy substrates. It packages, recirculates, and creates fatty acids and processes, stores, and creates glucose. If the liver becomes insulin-resistant, these processes are severely affected, resulting in significant metabolic consequences. When skeletal muscle develops insulin resistance, excess glucose in the blood is shunted to the liver. When the liver tissue senses an excess of energy substrate, particularly in the form of diacylglycerol, a process similar to that in skeletal muscle occurs. In the liver, the diacylglycerol content activates protein kinase C epsilon (PKC-epsilon), which decreases proximal insulin signaling. Excess glucose enters hepatocytes via insulin-independent pathways stimulating DNL via substrate push, creating more fatty acids from the glucose surplus. The excess fatty acid is deposited in the liver or as ectopic lipid throughout the viscera. Additionally, immune-mediated inflammatory changes contribute to excess lipolysis from adipose tissue, which is re-esterified by the liver and further adds to circulating fatty acid and ectopic lipid deposition. Finally, normal insulin-mediated suppression of gluconeogenesis is defective, and the liver continues to create more glucose, adding to the circulating glucose surplus. [25] [30]

agent1_snippets.title: Glucose Intolerance -- Pathophysiology
agent1_snippets.content: The two subsets of glucose intolerance, IFG and IGT differ in the site of insulin resistance as well as the pattern of insulin secretion. IFG is characterized predominantly by hepatic insulin resistance and normal muscle insulin sensitivity. People with IFG show a defect in early phase insulin secretory response to glucose. IGT is mainly associated with muscle insulin resistance. In addition to the defective early-phase insulin-secretory response, IGT also shows a severe deficit in late-phase insulin secretion. Evolving data suggest a role for impaired lipolysis, abnormal incretin biology, glucagon dysregulation and inflammation in both IGT and IFG Nathan DM 2007 [4] .

agent1_snippets.title: Insulin Resistance -- Prognosis
agent1_snippets.content: The prognosis of insulin resistance depends on the subset of the disease, the severity of the disease, underlying pancreatic beta-cell function, the heritable susceptibility of the patient to the secondary complications from insulin resistance, and individual response to appropriate therapy. The outcomes range from mildly insulin-resistant, asymptomatic individuals to those with catastrophic cardiovascular or cerebrovascular events and their resulting morbidity and mortality.

agent1_snippets.title: Fatty Liver -- Pathophysiology
agent1_snippets.content: Current concepts point to insulin resistance as the primary metabolic defect leading to NAFLD. Insulin resistance increases the influx of free fatty acids into the liver. This happens due to the failure of insulin to suppress the hormone-sensitive lipase, causing more FFA to be released from the adipose tissue. Also, elevated insulin levels and insulin resistance promote continuous synthesis of triglycerides in the liver. These 2 sources of triglycerides result in the accumulation of lipids in the hepatocytes, causing macrovesicular hepatic steatosis (see Image. Liver Biopsy, Hepatic Steatosis). [6] [10] Lipid accumulation in nonadipose tissue is a key factor for progressing insulin resistance, DM(Diabetes mellitus), and cardiovascular disease. [11] The current hypothesis is that NASH evolves from NAFLD caused by a second "hit." The evidence behind this second insult is inconclusive, but the most acceptable theories involve oxidative stress, specific cytokines, and lipopolysaccharides. Free fatty acids and hyperinsulinemia potentiate lipid peroxidation and the release of hydroxy-free radicals, directly injuring the hepatocytes by recruiting neuroinflammatory mediators. Chronic liver injury over time lead to activation of stellate cells, creating a potential for hepatic fibrosis. The diagnosis of NASH can only be established with a liver biopsy. Biopsy findings can reveal the following:

agent1_snippets.title: Biochemistry, C Peptide -- Clinical Significance -- Pearls
agent1_snippets.content: C-peptide is secreted in equimolar concentrations with insulin from the beta cells. It is a valid measure of insulin secretion especially following challenges with glucagon or a mixed meal. C-peptide is extremely useful in the differential diagnosis of hyperinsulinemic-hypoglycemia.

agent1_snippets.title: Physiology, Glucose -- Pathophysiology
agent1_snippets.content: Reactive hypoglycemia: Reactive hypoglycemia occurs postprandially due to excessive insulin secretion in response to carbohydrate-rich meals. This exaggerated insulin release leads to rapid glucose clearance, resulting in hypoglycemic episodes. Fasting hypoglycemia: Fasting hypoglycemia can stem from various etiologies, including hormonal deficiencies, liver disease, or metabolic enzyme deficiencies, disrupting the balance between glucose production and utilization during fasting periods. Metabolic syndrome: Metabolic syndrome encompasses a cluster of metabolic abnormalities, including central obesity, insulin resistance, dyslipidemia, and hypertension, predisposing individuals to cardiovascular disease and type 2 diabetes. Insulin resistance plays a central role in the pathogenesis of metabolic syndrome, driving aberrant glucose and lipid metabolism. [28] [29] [30]

agent1_snippets.title: Metabolic Dysfunction-Associated Steatotic Liver Disease (MΑSLD) -- Evaluation -- Assessment of comorbidities
agent1_snippets.content: More sophisticated insulin resistance indices, derived from oral glucose tolerance tests or mixed meal tests, are also available for comprehensive metabolic assessment. Dyslipidemia screening requires fasting plasma triglycerides, total cholesterol, LDL and HDL cholesterol measurements, with a one-time lipoprotein-a assessment, and may be supplemented by nonesterified fatty acids and apolipoprotein B quantification. Kidney disease assessment involves measuring plasma and urine creatinine, as well as serum and urine albumin levels, and calculating the estimated glomerular filtration rate.

agent1_snippets.title: Non-Diabetic Hypoglycemia -- Evaluation
agent1_snippets.content: Patients presenting with the features of Whipple’s triad require further evaluation for the underlying cause of hypoglycemia. Initial laboratory investigations include: Urea & electrolytes Liver function tests Fasting glucose Insulin, C-peptide, and proinsulin levels at the time of documented hypoglycemia Sulfonylurea and meglitinide screen Beta-hydroxybutyrate (BOHB) Early morning cortisol (if indeterminate, then a short Synacthen test should be arranged) and IGF-1 levels IGF-2 levels if there is a history of weight loss and malignancy Insulin/insulin receptor antibodies if elevated insulin levels/partially suppressed C-peptide

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 2 -- Community 1267
agent2_snippets.content: DISEASES OF THE ENDOCRINE PANCREAS INCLUDING DIABETES is a broad category that includes several specific pancreatic endocrine disorders. Among its children are ارتفاع السكر في الدم, which describes the elevated blood‑glucose levels that characterize hyperglycemia; مرض السكري دون مضاعفات, which refers to diabetes that has not yet produced secondary complications; فرط أنسولين الدم, indicating an excess of circulating insulin often seen in early insulin‑resistance states or insulin‑producing tumors; and ورم غلوكاغوني, a rare glucagon‑secreting tumor (glucagonoma) that can also cause hyperglycemia. These conditions are interrelated through their impact on pancreatic hormone production and regulation of blood glucose, highlighting the importance of comprehensive assessment and management of pancreatic endocrine function in patient care.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 4 -- Community 2721
agent2_snippets.content: DISEASES OF THE ENDOCRINE PANCREAS INCLUDING DIABETES is a broad category that includes ارتفاع السكر في الدم, اعتلال عصبي سكري, فرط أنسولين الدم, and ورم غلوكاغوني. ارتفاع السكر في الدم (high blood sugar) is a primary manifestation of diabetes, reflecting impaired glucose regulation by the endocrine pancreas. اعتلال عصبي سكري (diabetic neuropathy) is a common chronic complication of prolonged hyperglycemia, affecting peripheral nerves and leading to sensory and motor deficits. فرط أنسولين الدم (hyperinsulinemia) denotes excessive insulin secretion, which can occur in early insulin resistance or insulin‑producing tumors and may precede or accompany diabetes. ورم غلوكاغوني (glucagonoma) is a rare pancreatic neuroendocrine tumor that secretes excess glucagon, often resulting in persistent hyperglycemia and weight loss. Recognizing how these conditions are interrelated within endocrine pancreatic disease supports comprehensive diagnosis, monitoring, and therapeutic strategies for affected patients.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 1756
agent2_snippets.content: The category “DISEASES OF THE ENDOCRINE PANCREAS INCLUDING DIABETES” encompasses a spectrum of disorders and clinical manifestations that arise from dysfunction of the pancreatic endocrine system. It includes “ارتفاع السكر في الدم” (hyperglycemia), which represents elevated blood‑glucose levels and is a primary feature of diabetes. “غيبوبة فرط الأسمولية السكري” (diabetic hyperosmolar coma) is a severe, acute complication of uncontrolled hyperglycemia characterized by extreme dehydration and altered mental status. “مرض السكري دون مضاعفات” (diabetes mellitus without complications) denotes diabetes in its uncomplicated form, serving as the baseline condition from which other sequelae may develop. Among the chronic complications, “اعتلال عصبي سكري” (diabetic neuropathy) reflects nerve damage caused by prolonged hyperglycemia, leading to sensory and motor deficits. “ضمور نصف الوجه المترقي” (progressive hemifacial atrophy) is listed as a possible extra‑pancreatic manifestation that can be associated with underlying endocrine pancreatic disease. Metabolic dysregulation is further illustrated by “فرط أنسولين الدم” (hyperinsulinemia), an excess of circulating insulin that may precede or accompany insulin resistance. Finally, “ورم غلوكاغوني” (glucagonoma) represents a rare pancreatic neuroendocrine tumor that secretes excess glucagon, producing a distinct clinical syndrome. Together, these entities illustrate the diverse acute, chronic, and neoplastic conditions linked to diseases of the endocrine pancreas, highlighting the importance of comprehensive assessment and management of both glycemic control and associated complications.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 1930
agent2_snippets.content: DISEASES OF THE ENDOCRINE PANCREAS INCLUDING DIABETES is a broad classification that encompasses several distinct conditions. Within this category, مرض السكري دون مضاعفات refers to diabetes without complications, representing a form of the disease where hyperglycemia is present but secondary complications have not yet developed. فرط أنسولين الدم denotes hyperinsulinemia, a state of elevated insulin levels in the blood that can arise from pancreatic beta‑cell overactivity or insulin‑secreting tumors and may lead to hypoglycemia. ورم غلوكاغوني describes glucagonoma, a rare pancreatic neuroendocrine tumor that secretes excess glucagon, causing characteristic metabolic disturbances such as glucose intolerance, weight loss, and a distinctive skin rash. Together, these three entities illustrate the spectrum of disorders that fall under DISEASES OF THE ENDOCRINE PANCREAS INCLUDING DIABETES, highlighting the varied clinical presentations and management considerations associated with pancreatic endocrine pathology.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 1 -- Community 194
agent2_snippets.content: The classification “DISEASES OF THE ENDOCRINE PANCREAS INCLUDING DIABETES” is situated within the broader category of “other endocrine disorders.” Within this group, several specific conditions are identified as subtypes or manifestations: “ارتفاع السكر في الدم” (hyperglycemia) represents elevated blood glucose levels; “مرض السكري دون مضاعفات” denotes diabetes mellitus without complications; “نقص تركيز السكر” (hypoglycemia) reflects abnormally low blood glucose and is also recognized as a component of the wider “other endocrine disorders” category; “فرط أنسولين الدم” (hyperinsulinemia) describes excessive circulating insulin; “ورم غلوكاغوني” (glucagonoma) is a pancreatic tumor that secretes glucagon; and “اضطراب إفراز الجسترين” (gastrin secretion disorder) indicates abnormal gastrin release. The gastrin secretion disorder further leads to “الحثل وإضطرابات الإفراز في الغشاء المخاطي المَعِدِيّ المِعَوِيّ” (gastric mucosal atrophy and associated secretion abnormalities), illustrating a downstream effect on the gastric mucosa. Together, these relationships delineate how diabetes‑related endocrine pancreatic diseases encompass a spectrum of hyper‑ and hypoglycemic states, hormone‑excess conditions, neoplastic processes, and secondary gastrointestinal disturbances, all nested within the larger framework of endocrine disorders.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 1 -- Community 133
agent2_snippets.content: The community’s medical knowledge classifies drugs used in diabetes into two principal subclasses: insulins and analogues, and non‑insulin blood‑glucose‑lowering agents. Insulins and analogues include long‑acting injectable formulations such as insulin glargine, the fixed‑ratio combinations insulin degludec / liraglutide and insulin glargine / lixisenatide; liraglutide and lixisenatide also belong to the glucagon‑like peptide‑1 (GLP‑1) analogue class, which enhances glucose‑dependent insulin secretion. Non‑insulin agents are organized by mechanism: alpha‑glucosidase inhibitors delay carbohydrate absorption; dipeptidyl‑peptidase‑4 (DPP‑4) inhibitors—alogliptin, sitagliptin, and the combination simvastatin / sitagliptin—prolong incretin activity; sulfonylureas for blood‑glucose lowering—glibenclamide, glimepiride, and tolbutamide—stimulate pancreatic insulin release; biguanides, represented by metformin, reduce hepatic glucose production and improve peripheral insulin sensitivity. Tolbutamide is additionally listed among diagnostic agents for diabetes testing. This taxonomy links insulin‑based therapy with a diverse array of mechanistic drug classes and specific combinations, supporting individualized patient management in diabetes mellitus.

Mechanistically, insulin aspart exerts its glucose‑lowering effect by targeting the human insulin receptor (INSR protein), the primary receptor mediating insulin’s metabolic actions. This dual characterization highlights both its therapeutic role within mixed‑insulin therapy and its specific molecular interaction with the INSR protein, informing its use in achieving optimal glycemic control.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 1545
agent2_snippets.content: The category “DISEASES OF THE ENDOCRINE PANCREAS INCLUDING DIABETES” encompasses a spectrum of pancreatic and metabolic disorders. It includes “مرض السكري دون مضاعفات” (diabetes mellitus without complications) as the baseline form of the disease, as well as “مرض السكري نوع 2” (type 2 diabetes), which represents the most common adult‑onset variant characterized by insulin resistance. Severe acute metabolic decompensation can manifest as “غيبوبة فرط الأسمولية السكري” (diabetic hyperosmolar coma), a life‑threatening state of extreme hyperglycemia and dehydration. Chronic complications of diabetes are reflected in “اعتلال عصبي سكري” (diabetic neuropathy), a progressive nerve damage that can cause pain, sensory loss, and autonomic dysfunction. Hormonal dysregulation associated with pancreatic endocrine tumors is represented by “فرط أنسولين الدم” (hyperinsulinemia), often due to insulin‑secreting neoplasms, and “ورم غلوكاغوني” (glucagonoma), a rare tumor that overproduces glucagon leading to characteristic skin and metabolic findings. Additionally, the list includes “ضمور نصف الوجه المترقي” (progressive facial hemi‑atrophy), a condition that, while not a classic diabetic complication, may be observed in the broader context of endocrine pancreatic disease. Together, these entities illustrate the diverse clinical presentations—from uncomplicated hyperglycemia to acute metabolic crises, chronic neuropathic damage, and endocrine neoplasms—associated with diseases of the endocrine pancreas, underscoring the importance of comprehensive evaluation and management in patient care.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 3 -- Community 1452
agent2_snippets.content: DISEASES OF THE ENDOCRINE PANCREAS INCLUDING DIABETES is a broad category that encompasses several specific conditions. Among its subentities are اعتلال عصبي سكري (diabetic neuropathy), a chronic complication in which prolonged hyperglycemia leads to damage of peripheral nerves and results in sensory loss, pain, or autonomic dysfunction. The category also includes فرط أنسولين الدم (hyperinsulinemia), a state of elevated insulin levels in the bloodstream that can arise from insulin resistance, early type 2 diabetes, or insulin‑secreting tumors and contributes to metabolic disturbances such as hypoglycemia and weight gain. Another child condition is ورم غلوكاغوني (glucagonoma), a rare neoplasm of the pancreatic α‑cells that secretes excess glucagon, producing a characteristic syndrome of hyperglycemia, necrolytic migratory erythema, weight loss, and anemia. Finally, the category lists ضمور نصف الوجه المترقي (progressive hemifacial atrophy), an uncommon, often autoimmune‑mediated disorder characterized by gradual wasting of the skin, subcutaneous tissue, and muscle on one side of the face; its inclusion reflects the recognition that endocrine pancreatic diseases, particularly those with autoimmune components, may be associated with extra‑pancreatic manifestations. Together, these entities illustrate the diverse clinical spectrum—from metabolic and neuropathic complications to neoplastic and autoimmune manifestations—linked to diseases of the endocrine pancreas, underscoring the importance of comprehensive evaluation and multidisciplinary management in affected patients.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 3 -- Community 1766
agent2_snippets.content: DISEASES OF THE ENDOCRINE PANCREAS INCLUDING DIABETES is classified as a subgroup of other endocrine disorders. Within this pancreatic endocrine disease category are several specific conditions: ارتفاع السكر في الدم (hyperglycemia or elevated blood glucose), نقص تركيز السكر (hypoglycemia or low blood glucose), اعتلال عصبي سكري (diabetic neuropathy), فرط أنسولين الدم (hyperinsulinemia or excess insulin in the blood), and ورم غلوكاغوني (glucagonoma, a tumor that secretes excess glucagon). These entities together represent the spectrum of metabolic disturbances, chronic complications, and neoplastic processes that arise from dysfunction of the pancreatic endocrine cells. Hyperglycemia and hypoglycemia illustrate opposite dysregulations of glucose homeostasis, while hyperinsulinemia reflects overproduction of insulin and glucagonoma reflects overproduction of glucagon. Diabetic neuropathy is a long‑term complication that develops from persistent hyperglycemia. All of these conditions are linked under the broader umbrella of other endocrine disorders, highlighting their common basis in hormone imbalance. Although one relationship suggested that نقص تركيز السكر might be a parent of other endocrine disorders, the more coherent hierarchy places DISEASES OF THE ENDOCRINE PANCREAS INCLUDING DIABETES as the child of other endocrine disorders, with نقص تركيز السكر correctly understood as one of its constituent manifestations.
